"Intouchables" is a very unique movie. If I were to describe all the events that happen in the movie, you would think it was a TV mini-series full of episodic misadventures. But, with the way the movie edits its scenes, it moves along at a clip, never wasting a minute. Hearing that, you may think that the film is action packed. But no, it is a very mellow movie. The film is at its strongest when our two main characters are just hanging out. Whenever Philipe and Driss are sharing screentime together, it is a good time. It is whenever the movie jumps to a conflict it is less interesting. I'm glad to see that so many people enjoyed this relatively small French film from 2011. I genuinely wish I would have liked it more, I just found the conflicts with the kid, the egg and the family to be draining. But the time our two leads are just hanging out, it is a real good time.

There is something so fatuous about the whole farrago that one will never again believe the old saw that the French are more cultured than the Brits and couldn't possibly be taken for this sort of gooey ride.

In "The Intouchables," feeling he has no chance at the job in question, all Driss(Omar Sy) wants to do is get his form signed, so he can continue with benefits after spending six months in prison. So, Magalie(Audrey Fleurot), the major domo to Philippe(Francois Cluzet), a super wealthy paraplegic, tells him to return the following day. When he does, Driss is amazed to learn he has the job to take care of Philippe in his daily activities, not to mention the living quarters that go along with it. But that's the easy part. The hard part is making it through the trial period. Let's be honest. We've seen hundreds of movies like "The Intouchables" before and we will see hundreds more before it is all over. Except maybe not as patronizing or cliched as this one, with Margalie suffering worst in this category. For example, tough love is not the solution for every problem as I think everybody wants to work but can't due to a lack of jobs, especially ex-convicts. Yes, Philippe does bring up his ill-gotten wealth a couple of times and the classic music medley is a definite highlight. But that's kind of too little, too late to save this disappointing movie. And not to compare bagels to croissants, but I think the television series "Suits" did it much better in the giving somebody a chance department.

1+1 (also known as The Intouchables) is a 2011 French comedy-drama film directed by Olivier Nakache and Éric Toledano. The film is based on the true story of Philippe Pozzo di Borgo, a wealthy French aristocrat who became a quadriplegic after a paragliding accident, and his relationship with his caregiver, Driss Bassiri, a young man from a poor neighborhood in Paris. The film was a critical and commercial success, becoming the highest-grossing French film of all time. It was also nominated for nine César Awards, including Best Film and Best Director. What I liked about the film: The acting is superb. François Cluzet and Omar Sy give two of the best performances of their careers. The story is heartwarming and inspiring. It is a story about friendship, hope, and overcoming adversity. The film is funny and touching. It will make you laugh and cry. The film is beautifully shot. The cinematography is stunning. What I didn't like about the film: The film is a bit long. The film can be a bit sentimental at times. Overall, 1+1 is a beautiful and inspiring film that is sure to please audiences of all ages. It is a film about the power of friendship, hope, and the human spirit. I highly recommend this film to anyone who enjoys a good comedy-drama.

Title: A Heartwarming Tale of Friendship and Triumph: Review of "The Intouchables" (2011) "The Intouchables" is a touching and uplifting film that celebrates the transformative power of friendship and human connection. Directed by Olivier Nakache and Éric Toledano, this French comedy-drama tells the inspiring true story of the unlikely bond between Philippe, a wealthy quadriplegic, and Driss, his unconventional caregiver. Set against the backdrop of Paris, "The Intouchables" follows Philippe, played by François Cluzet, as he navigates life from the confines of his wheelchair. Despite his physical limitations, Philippe maintains a sharp wit and zest for life, but he finds himself in need of assistance after a paragliding accident leaves him paralyzed from the neck down. Enter Driss, portrayed by Omar Sy, a charismatic and streetwise immigrant from the outskirts of Paris. In need of a job to maintain his welfare benefits, Driss applies for the position of Philippe's caregiver with little intention of actually fulfilling the duties. However, his irreverent attitude and irrepressible spirit captivate Philippe, and the two form an unlikely bond that transcends their differences. "The Intouchables" is a testament to the power of empathy and understanding, as Philippe and Driss learn to see past societal labels and embrace each other as equals. Through their shared experiences and misadventures, they discover the true meaning of friendship and find joy in the simple pleasures of life. The performances in "The Intouchables" are nothing short of remarkable. François Cluzet delivers a nuanced portrayal of Philippe, capturing both his vulnerability and resilience with authenticity and grace. Meanwhile, Omar Sy shines as Driss, infusing the character with warmth, humor, and a genuine sense of humanity. In addition to its heartfelt performances, "The Intouchables" is also a visually stunning film. The picturesque streets of Paris provide a beautiful backdrop for the story, while the cinematography captures both the grandeur of Philippe's mansion and the bustling energy of the city. It's so rare to find something like this nowadays. It's a masterpiece! It was nice to see that there are still such movies, which can stay in your head for a long time, and not immediately after the end. Also, it's very rare where everything comes together perfectly: the music, the actors, the script. It's all great! The plot seems to be simple: a disabled man who was already disappointed in his life; a disabled man whom everybody pities and nobody believes in. But how could it not be so! Along the way Philippa meets a desperate, funny and so kind man - Driss. It seemed that they would never be friends, and in general ... What do such people have in common? But the movie answers the question: they have a lot to learn from each other. Driss helps Philippe to enjoy life, as he is the only one who can forget about compassion and just perceive him as a full and healthy person. And at the same time, Philippe helps Driss to find himself, to realize that he too is worth something. I would like to note the play of actors. Not only the main characters, but also the minor ones. All of them look very harmonious and very 'cozy'. Of course, François Cluzet and Omar Sy played just flawlessly. Not for a second it was impossible to imagine that it was just a play. Another very memorable thing is the music. It's just beautiful! It's so well chosen. How perfectly the emotions are conveyed through it. After the movie you want to listen to all the classics again and just enjoy it. In the end, the movie turned out to be light, sweet, like a our purplr friend Grimace, with a philosophical beginning and somewhere even a little sad. I would advise everyone to watch it, because it really can leave such a warm and pleasant imprint on the heart.
It's an example of smart and capable filmmaking that can make you laugh as well as tug at your heartstrings. P.Ssss. And I advise you to check Grimace community.
